1:
"There was a certain man in Caesarea called Cornelius, a centurion of the band called the Italian band," The witnesses note the introduction of Cornelius, the first Gentile convert, and the significance of the moment. Caesarea was the Roman capital of Judea, the seat of the governor; the "Italian band" was a cohort of Roman soldiers. Cornelius, a centurion (commander of a hundred), was a Gentile officer of the occupying power — yet a devout man (v.2).

The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the epochal character of this chapter. The conversion of Cornelius is the opening of the door of faith to the Gentiles — the great turning-point at which the gospel, hitherto confined to Jews and proselytes, breaks the barrier and is offered freely to the uncircumcised. Chrysostom and the patristic voices mark its place in the history of redemption.

The choice of Cornelius the witnesses read as providential. A devout, God-fearing centurion, of good report among the Jews (v.22), was the fittest connecting link between Jew and Gentile — neither a profligate pagan nor a circumcised proselyte, but a Gentile of pure life and sincere piety, against whom the Jews could not easily object when God poured his grace upon him.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the great chapter. The witnesses agree: Cornelius, a centurion of the Italian band at Caesarea, was a Gentile officer of the occupying power — yet, as the next verse shows, a devout and God-fearing man. The verse opens the epochal account of the conversion of the Gentiles: the door of faith now flung open to the uncircumcised, the gospel breaking the barrier between Jew and Gentile (Henry). The choice of Cornelius was providential: a Gentile of pure life and sincere piety, of good report among the Jews, the fittest link between the two peoples, against whom the Jews could not object when God poured his grace upon him. The verse marks the beginning of the great step that would, through Peter, open the church to all nations — the fulfillment of the vision of the sheet (vv.11-16) and the gospel's advance beyond Israel. The conversion of one God-fearing centurion would settle, by the manifest gift of the Spirit (v.44), the question of the Gentiles' admission to the church without circumcision — the question that would dominate the apostolic age. The narrative of Cornelius is, with Pentecost, one of the two great pivots of Acts: the gospel offered first to the Jew, now to the Gentile.
2:
"A devout man, and one that feared God with all his house, which gave much alms to the people, and prayed to God alway." The witnesses note the singular piety of Cornelius. The grammatical voices identify his religious status. Robertson: "Luke describes a God-fearing proselyte... the Gentile seekers after God who had come into the worship of the synagogue without circumcision, and were not strictly proselytes." Clarke: "a proselyte of the gate, though not a proselyte of justice, because he had not entered into the bond of the covenant by circumcision" — "so much of a Jew and so much of a Gentile, to form the connecting link between both people."

The witnesses dwell on his virtues. Barnes notes the marks of piety: devout, fearing God, instructing his household, giving much alms, praying always. Ellicott notes "with all his house" — Cornelius sought to impart his faith to his soldiers and slaves. His alms were given "to the people," the Jews, showing his sympathy with their religion.

The Reformed Calvin draws the pattern of a godly life: "we have a way to live well described in his person... let faith and religion be the foundation, which being taken away, all other virtues are nothing else but smokes." He praises Cornelius's family religion ("he had a church in his house") and rebukes the coldness of professing Christians by his example: "if a small sparkle of faith prevailed so much in him, what ought the full brightness of knowledge to work in us?"

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius was a man of singular piety — devout, God-fearing, instructing his whole household, giving much alms to the Jews, and praying always. His status was that of a God-fearer or proselyte of the gate: a Gentile who worshipped the true God in the synagogue without circumcision (Robertson, Clarke), the fit connecting link between Jew and Gentile. Calvin draws from him the pattern of a godly life (faith the foundation of all virtue), praises his family religion, and rebukes the coldness of Christians by the warmth of this Gentile's piety. The verse displays the rare and beautiful character of Cornelius: a soldier of the occupying power, yet a man of prayer, charity, and the fear of God, who led his household in religion. His piety, though as yet without full knowledge of Christ, was sincere and the fruit of grace already working in him (v.4). The verse sets before the church the model of a devout life — and the providential preparation of the first Gentile convert, whose evident godliness would silence the objections of the circumcised when God poured his Spirit upon him.
3:
"He saw in a vision evidently about the ninth hour of the day an angel of God coming in to him, and saying unto him, Cornelius." The witnesses note the angelic vision and its clear, waking character. The grammatical voices stress that this was no dream or trance but an open vision. Wesley: "Not in a trance, like Peter: plainly, so as to leave one not accustomed to things of this kind no room to suspect any imposition." Ellicott distinguishes it from a dream or a trance.

On the ninth hour, the witnesses note it was the hour of evening prayer. Barnes: "About three o'clock, P.M. This was the usual hour of evening worship among the Jews." Cornelius, observing the Jewish hours of prayer, was at his devotions when the angel appeared — the vision answering his prayer (Ellicott).

On the importance of the occasion, Barnes notes the significance of an angelic visitation: "The importance of the occasion — the introduction of the gospel to a Gentile, and hence to the entire Gentile world — was probably the chief reason why an angel was commissioned to visit the Roman centurion." The Reformed Calvin notes the time is given to remove all suspicion of illusion: "it was fair daylight when the angel appeared, that the vision might be more evident."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius saw, in a clear waking vision (not a dream or trance, Wesley, Ellicott), an angel of God at the ninth hour — the hour of evening prayer, while he was at his devotions (Barnes). The vision answered his prayer (Ellicott). The clear, daylight character of the vision is noted to remove all suspicion of illusion (Calvin); and the dispatch of an angel marks the importance of the occasion — the introduction of the gospel to the Gentile world (Barnes). The verse displays the divine initiative in the conversion of Cornelius: God, who had prepared his heart by grace, now sends an angel to direct him to Peter. The vision, given at the hour of prayer to a man at prayer, is God's answer to a seeking soul. The verse marks the first of the two converging visions (Cornelius's and Peter's) by which God orchestrates the meeting that would open the door to the Gentiles. The angel calls Cornelius by name — the personal address of a God who knows and answers his devout worshipper, and who is about to grant him the fuller light of the gospel.
4:
"And when he looked on him, he was afraid, and said, What is it, Lord? And he said unto him, Thy prayers and thine alms are come up for a memorial before God." The witnesses note Cornelius's fear and the acceptance of his prayers — and divide on a great theological question. On the "memorial," the grammatical voices note the sacrificial language. Ellicott: "The word so used was emphatically sacrificial and liturgical (Leviticus 2:2)... the prayers and alms were accepted as a true sacrifice, more acceptable than the blood of bulls and goats." Barnes: "They were an evidence of piety towards God, and were accepted as such."

The great question: how could his works be accepted before faith in Christ? Ellicott answers: "the good works were wrought by the power of God's grace already working in him. He was believing in the Light that lighteth every man... He had the faith which from the beginning of the world has justified (Hebrews 11:6)." Wesley: "every one who seeks faith in Christ, should seek it in prayer and doing good to all men."

The Reformed Calvin strongly rejects the Papist reading of works as preparation or merit: "he could obtain nothing by prayer unless faith went before... the fear of God and godliness do plainly prove that he was regenerate by the Spirit." Cornelius's works were the fruits of a faith already given, not a meritorious preparation; and faith cannot be separated from Christ — Cornelius, like the old fathers, "hoped for salvation of the Redeemer before he was revealed," having heard something of the promised Mediator.

The witnesses agree: Cornelius, afraid at the angel's appearance, heard that his prayers and alms had come up "for a memorial before God" — accepted as a true sacrifice (the sacrificial language, Ellicott). On the great question — how works could be accepted before clear faith in Christ — the witnesses agree they were the fruits of grace already working in him, not a meritorious preparation: he believed in the Light (Ellicott), was regenerate by the Spirit (Calvin), and hoped for the promised Redeemer though not yet clearly known. Calvin strongly rejects the Papist reading of works as preparation or merit. The verse warns, the witnesses note (Barnes, Clarke), against the misuse of Cornelius's example by the merely moral: he was not depending on works but was a man of sincere heart-piety, ready to embrace the Savior when offered. The verse displays God's acceptance of the devout seeker: the prayers and alms of a God-fearing Gentile, wrought by grace, were remembered before God, who answered them with the fuller light of the gospel. Acceptance rests not on merit but on the faith and grace already given — and is crowned with the knowledge of Christ.
5:
"And now send men to Joppa, and call for one Simon, whose surname is Peter:" The witnesses note the angel's direction to send for Peter — and the great principle that God uses human ministry. The grammatical voices note the verb "send for" (fetch for oneself). The Reformed Calvin draws out the providence and the lesson: "God dealt most favorably with Cornelius, in that he doth not command him to go himself, but to send messengers unto Peter, that he may stay quietly at home, and that Peter may endure the toil of the journey for his sake."

On why the angel does not himself teach Cornelius, Calvin gives the key lesson: "this seemeth an inconvenient thing that he resigneth his office to a mortal man... As when Christ appeared to Paul by a vision, he set Ananias to teach him, that he might by such an example establish the ministry of the preaching of the gospel... so now the angel giveth place to Peter." God will be heard through his appointed human ministers, not by visions or revelations bypassing the word.

Calvin draws the warning against those who despise preaching for private revelations: "we see how sore God hath punished their furious pride, who, contemning preaching, have looked for revelations from heaven. For since God will be heard in men, the ministers to whom he hath given his word cannot be contemned without contempt of him." Faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel directed Cornelius to send for Peter at Joppa — and the great lesson, drawn by Calvin, is that God uses human ministry: the angel does not teach Cornelius himself but sends him to a mortal man, that the ministry of preaching might be honoured (as Ananias was sent to Paul). God will be heard through his appointed ministers, not by revelations bypassing the word; and those who despise preaching for private revelations are sorely punished. The verse displays the divine method in conversion: not the angel but the apostle is to teach Cornelius, for faith comes by hearing (Romans 10:17). The verse also shows God's favour to Cornelius — he need not undertake the journey, but Peter is brought to him. The verse marks the second stage of the converging providence: the God-fearing Gentile directed to the apostle, that the gospel might be preached to him by a human voice. The honour God puts on the ministry of the word — sending an apostle rather than an angel — teaches the church to reverence the preaching of the gospel as the ordained means of faith and salvation.
6:
"He lodgeth with one Simon a tanner, whose house is by the sea side: he shall tell thee what thou oughtest to do." The witnesses note the precise direction and the purpose of Peter's coming. The grammatical voices note the location: Simon the tanner's house by the seaside (the tanner's trade requiring water and, being unclean to Jews, situated outside the city). Robertson notes some tanneries are by the seashore at Jaffa today.

On "what thou oughtest to do," the witnesses note Cornelius's seeking. Clarke: "matters of great moment had occupied the mind of Cornelius. He was not satisfied with the state of his own soul, nor with the degree he possessed of religious knowledge... perhaps he had heard of Jesus, and had been perplexed... and now prayed to God that he might know what part he should take." The clause (textually uncertain here but found at 11:14) shows the purpose: Peter would tell him the way of salvation.

The precise direction the witnesses read as confirming the divine origin of the vision. The exact naming of the house and host (Simon the tanner, by the sea) gave Cornelius certainty that this was a divine communication, to be obeyed without doubt. The verse marks the providential placing of Peter at the tanner's house (9:43), where the messengers would find him.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel directed Cornelius precisely — Peter lodges with Simon the tanner by the seaside — and Peter would tell him "what thou oughtest to do." The precise naming of host and place confirmed the divine origin of the vision (to be obeyed without doubt), and the providential placing of Peter at the tanner's house (9:43) is where the messengers would find him. The clause "what thou oughtest to do" (found at 11:14) shows Cornelius's seeking: a soul unsatisfied with its present knowledge, perhaps having heard of Jesus, praying to know the way of salvation (Clarke). The verse displays both the exactness of God's direction and the purpose of Peter's coming: to declare to the seeking Cornelius the gospel of Christ. The tanner's house — where a strict Jew might hesitate to lodge — providentially placed Peter for this meeting, and foreshadows the breaking of the clean/unclean barrier that the chapter unfolds. The God-fearing Gentile, directed to the apostle, awaits the word that will tell him what he must do to be saved — and the answer (vv.34-43) is faith in Christ for the remission of sins.
7:
"And when the angel which spake unto Cornelius was departed, he called two of his household servants, and a devout soldier of them that waited on him continually;" The witnesses note Cornelius's prompt obedience and the piety of his attendants. On the obedience, Calvin: "Luke declareth here how ready Cornelius was to obey when as he maketh no tarriance, but doth that with all diligence which he was commanded to do. And this was the cause that he was so forward, because he believed the promise."

On the "devout soldier," the witnesses note the piety Cornelius had instilled in those around him. Ellicott: "the man was, like his superior officer, a convert to the faith of Israel." Barnes: "an instance of the effect of piety in a military officer... even the labours and disadvantages of a camp are not necessarily hostile to the existence of piety." Cornelius's household and even his soldiers shared his devotion.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on godliness in every calling, including the soldier's: "there is no kind of life which excuseth us but that we must worship God purely. For a soldier's life was at that time most corrupt... and yet the Spirit of God beareth record in this place of the godliness of soldiers." He notes that these soldiers, embracing Christ, did not forsake their calling — refuting those who say it is unlawful for Christians to bear arms.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius, the angel departed, promptly obeyed — sending two household servants and a devout soldier (Calvin notes his forwardness sprang from faith in the promise). The piety of his attendants displays the influence of his godliness: his household and even his soldiers shared his devotion (Ellicott, Barnes), showing that piety can flourish even in a camp. Calvin draws the lesson that no calling excuses us from worshipping God purely — the soldier's life, though then most corrupt, is here adorned with godliness; and these devout soldiers, embracing Christ, did not forsake their calling, refuting those who deny that Christians may bear arms. The verse displays both Cornelius's prompt obedience and the fruit of his household religion: trustworthy, godly servants and a devout soldier ready to do his bidding. The verse marks the providential reward of Cornelius's family piety (v.2): he had faithful, God-fearing attendants to send on this errand. The conversion of Cornelius is thus also a testimony to the power of a godly life to influence all within its reach — household, servants, and soldiers alike drawn toward the fear of God.
8:
"And when he had declared all these things unto them, he sent them to Joppa." The brief verse notes the sending of the messengers and a providential parallel. The witnesses note that Cornelius explained the whole vision to his messengers. Gill: "partly to engage them the more cheerfully to go on the errand, and partly that they might be able to give a distinct relation of it to Peter." Calvin: he made known so great a matter to those whom he had instructed well before, that they might give ear to a commandment of God.

On the providential parallel with Jonah, the witnesses note the striking coincidence. Barnes and Clarke: "from Joppa Jonah was sent to preach to the Gentiles at Nineveh; and from the same place Peter was sent to preach to the Gentiles at Caesarea." The same city that sent the reluctant Jonah to the Gentiles now sends Peter — a fitting echo, given that Peter, like Jonah, would need to overcome his reluctance toward the Gentiles.

The sending of the messengers the witnesses read as the obedience of faith. Cornelius, having believed the vision, acts at once, sending his trusted attendants to fetch Peter. The verse completes the first half of the converging providence: the Gentile's messengers on their way to the apostle.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius explained the whole vision to his messengers (that they might go cheerfully and relate it faithfully to Peter, Gill) and sent them to Joppa — the obedience of faith, acting at once on the vision. The witnesses note the striking providential parallel: from Joppa Jonah was sent to the Gentiles at Nineveh, and from the same Joppa Peter is now summoned to the Gentiles at Caesarea (Barnes, Clarke) — a fitting echo, since Peter, like Jonah, would need to overcome reluctance toward the Gentiles. The verse completes the first stage of the converging providence: the Gentile's trusted, God-fearing messengers on their way to the apostle. The verse displays Cornelius's prompt and full obedience: he hides nothing, but makes the whole matter known, that the errand may be done in faith. The God who arranged the two visions now sets the messengers in motion toward Joppa, where Peter, on the housetop, is about to receive his own vision (v.9) preparing him to receive them. The providence converges; the door to the Gentiles is about to open.
9:
"On the morrow, as they went on their journey, and drew nigh unto the city, Peter went up upon the housetop to pray about the sixth hour:" The witnesses note the providential timing of Peter's prayer as the messengers approached. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the housetop as a place of prayer (the flat Eastern roof) and the sixth hour (noon) as a time of devotion. Peter's habit of prayer is again displayed.

On the providential timing, the witnesses note the convergence of events. Just as the messengers from Cornelius drew near the city, Peter went up to pray and received his vision — the two halves of God's plan meeting precisely. The grammatical voices note Luke's care in marking the timing, the natural and supernatural bordering closely.

The Reformed Calvin (under v.10) notes the preparation of Peter's mind by the trance for the heavenly oracle. The verse opens the account of Peter's vision, which would prepare him to receive the Gentile messengers and to go to Cornelius — the second of the two converging visions.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens Peter's vision. The witnesses agree: as the messengers from Cornelius drew near the city, Peter went up on the housetop to pray at the sixth hour (noon) — the providential timing of the two halves of God's plan meeting precisely (Henry). Peter's habit of prayer is again displayed; and the housetop, the flat Eastern roof, was a place of retirement for devotion. The verse opens the account of Peter's vision of the sheet (vv.11-16), the second of the two converging visions by which God orchestrates the meeting. The verse displays the providence that brings the praying apostle and the approaching messengers together at the appointed hour: Peter, at prayer, is about to receive the vision that will overturn his scruples and prepare him to carry the gospel to the Gentiles. The natural and the supernatural border closely (Furneaux): a man at noonday prayer, hungry, is made the channel of a revelation that would change the history of the church. The verse marks the moment when God begins to teach Peter, by the vision of the sheet, the lesson that Cornelius's messengers would apply: that nothing God has cleansed is to be called common, and that the Gentiles are no longer to be shunned as unclean.
10:
"And he became very hungry, and would have eaten: but while they made ready, he fell into a trance," The witnesses note Peter's hunger and the trance, and how the vision suited his bodily state. The grammatical voices explain the trance (ecstasy). Barnes: "a state of mind when the attention is absorbed in a particular train of thought, so that the external senses are partially or entirely suspended... the soul seems to have passed out of the body." Robertson: "An ecstasy came upon him, in which trance he passed out of himself."

On the hunger, the witnesses note how the vision (of food) suited Peter's condition. Wesley: "The symbols in visions and trances are generally suited to the state of the natural faculties." Ellicott: the trance "might in part be the natural consequence of the protracted fast, and the intense prayer." The hunger prepared the symbolism of the sheet full of animals.

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose of the trance: "Because our minds are wholly set upon the earth, to the end Peter might the better comprehend the oracle, it was meet that his mind should be carried up; and by this means was he prepared to receive the oracle, when as he is carried up above the world." The trance lifted Peter's mind above earthly things to receive the heavenly revelation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter, very hungry, fell into a trance (ecstasy) while the meal was prepared — a state in which the external senses were suspended and the soul absorbed in the vision (Barnes, Robertson). The hunger suited the symbolism of the vision (the sheet of animals): the symbols of visions are suited to the natural state (Wesley), and the trance arose in part from the fast and intense prayer (Ellicott). Calvin notes the purpose: the trance lifted Peter's mind above earthly things to receive the heavenly oracle. The verse displays the providence that prepared Peter, through hunger and trance, for the vision that would change his views: his bodily hunger became the occasion of a revelation about clean and unclean food, and through it, about Jew and Gentile. The verse marks the onset of the great vision: the apostle, lifted out of himself, is about to see the sheet let down from heaven, and to hear the voice that would overturn his lifelong scruples. The natural hunger and the supernatural trance together open the way for the lesson God would teach — that nothing he has cleansed is common.
11:
"And saw heaven opened, and a certain vessel descending unto him, as it had been a great sheet knit at the four corners, and let down to the earth:" The witnesses note the vision of the great sheet. The grammatical voices note the imagery. Clarke suggests it represents the universe and its nations: "Perhaps intended to be an emblem of the universe, and its various nations, to the four corners of which the Gospel was to extend, and to offer its blessings to all the inhabitants, without distinction." Robertson notes the sheet held by four cords, recalling Isaiah 11:12 (Israel gathered from the four corners).

On the descent from heaven, Barnes notes its significance: "Its descending from heaven may have been an intimation that that religion which was about to abolish the distinction between the Jews and other nations was of Divine origin." Ellicott connects the vision to Peter's prayer: one looking from Joppa toward the Gentile isles might seek to know how they would be brought into the fold.

The grammatical voices note the careful "as it had been" — the vision was a representation, not a literal object. Barnes (under v.11): "The sacred writer does not say that Peter literally saw such an object descending; but he uses this as an imperfect description of the vision." The verse opens the symbolic revelation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter saw heaven opened and a great sheet, knit at the four corners, let down to the earth. The imagery suggests the universe and its nations — the four corners to which the gospel was to extend, offering its blessings to all without distinction (Clarke), recalling Isaiah 11:12. The descent from heaven intimates the divine origin of the religion about to abolish the Jew/Gentile distinction (Barnes). Ellicott connects the vision to Peter's prayer, seeking how the Gentiles would be brought into the fold. The verse opens the symbolic revelation that would teach Peter the lesson of the chapter: the sheet, descending from heaven and containing all manner of creatures, is the emblem of God's purpose to gather all nations into one church. The verse displays the divine method of teaching by vision: the apostle, in his trance, is shown a heavenly sheet full of clean and unclean animals, the meaning of which (vv.13-15) would overturn his scruples and open his heart to the Gentiles. The sheet let down from heaven and taken back up again is the parable of God's gracious purpose to receive all peoples — Jew and Gentile alike — into the fellowship of the gospel.
12:
"Wherein were all manner of fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air." The witnesses note the contents of the sheet — clean and unclean animals together. The grammatical voices note that both kinds were present. Clarke: "Consequently, both the clean and unclean were present in this visionary representation: those that the Jewish law allowed to be sacrificed to God, or proper for food; as well as those which that law had prohibited." Robertson: "Both clean and unclean animals are in the sheet."

The Reformed Calvin gives the key interpretation: the vision teaches that the distinction between clean and unclean is abolished, and so the distinction between Jew and Gentile: "the difference which God had made in times past is now taken away. And as he had put difference between living creatures, so, having chosen to himself one people, he counted all nations unclean... Now the difference between living creatures being taken away, he teacheth by the consequent that there is no such disagreement among men any longer."

Calvin draws out the breaking of the middle wall: "God made the covenant of life common to all the whole world, which he had shut up in one nation, as in a treasure... it is as much as if God should pronounce from heaven that he receiveth all people of the world into favor." The mingling of clean and unclean animals is the emblem of the union of Jew and Gentile in one church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sheet contained all manner of animals, clean and unclean together (Clarke, Robertson). Calvin gives the key interpretation: as the distinction between clean and unclean animals is abolished, so is the distinction between Jew and Gentile — God now making the covenant of life common to all the world, which he had shut up in one nation. The mingling of clean and unclean creatures in one sheet is the emblem of the union of Jew and Gentile in one church (the breaking of the middle wall, Ephesians 2:14). The verse displays the heart of the vision's meaning: God receives all peoples into favour, the old separation done away. The unclean animals, let down from heaven in the same sheet as the clean, teach Peter that the Gentiles, formerly shunned as unclean, are now to be received. The verse prepares for the command (v.13) and the lesson (v.15): the apostle, shown the clean and unclean together, is being taught that he must no longer call any man common or unclean. The sheet of mingled creatures is the visible parable of the gospel's universal reach — Jew and Gentile gathered into one body by the grace of God.
13:
"And there came a voice to him, Rise, Peter; kill, and eat." The witnesses note the command and its meaning. On the verb "kill," the grammatical voices note it can mean "sacrifice." Clarke prefers the sacrificial sense: "Sacrifice and eat... Animals that were offered in sacrifice were considered as given to God." He draws out the symbolism: the Jews and Gentiles, represented by clean and unclean animals, were to be offered up as a spiritual sacrifice to God, with Peter as the instrument, opening the door of faith to the Gentiles.

The Reformed Calvin prefers the plain sense: "Whereas some pick an allegory out of the word kill... I do not prosecute that, but plainness pleaseth me better, that God doth take away by this voice the law concerning the choice of beasts, that he may also teach that he rejecteth no people." The voice from heaven (like the sheet) marks the command as from God.

The command the witnesses read as the divine abrogation of the food laws and, through them, the lesson about the Gentiles. The voice bids Peter eat what the law had forbidden — a command that would scandalize his scruple (v.14), but that teaches the abolition of the old distinction.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the voice commanded Peter to rise, kill, and eat. On "kill," Clarke prefers the sacrificial sense (the clean and unclean animals representing Jew and Gentile, to be offered as a spiritual sacrifice through Peter's ministry), while Calvin prefers the plain sense (the abrogation of the food laws, teaching that God rejects no people). The voice from heaven, like the sheet, marks the command as divine. The verse displays the heart of the vision: the apostle bidden to eat what the law forbade — a command that would scandalize his lifelong scruple (v.14), yet that teaches the abolition of the old distinction between clean and unclean, and through it, between Jew and Gentile. The command, repugnant to Peter's training, is the means by which God overturns his prejudice and prepares him to receive the Gentile messengers. The verse marks the divine initiative in breaking down the barrier: God himself commands Peter to set aside the ceremonial law, that the way may be opened to the Gentiles. The voice that bids him "kill and eat" is the voice that bids the church receive all nations into the fellowship of the gospel.
14:
"But Peter said, Not so, Lord; for I have never eaten any thing that is common or unclean." The witnesses note Peter's scruple and his refusal. The grammatical voices note the character of the refusal. Robertson: "It is a polite refusal with a reason given... Peter recognizes the invitation to slay the unclean animals as from the Lord but declines it." Ellicott notes the consistency with Peter's character: "the emphatic resistance even to a voice from heaven is strikingly in harmony with the features of St. Peter's character" (cf. "Be it far from thee, Lord," Matthew 16:22; "Thou shalt never wash my feet," John 13:8).

On "common or unclean," Barnes explains: "common" (opposed to sacred, i.e. profane) and "unclean" (forbidden by the ceremonial law). Peter pleads his lifelong observance of the law (Leviticus 11): "this was a law of God, and as it was unrepealed, he did not dare to act in a different manner from what it required."

The Reformed Calvin notes the conflict between the law and the vision: "There was a certain show of repugnancy between the law and the vision; therefore Peter is not hasty, but desireth first to have his doubt dissolved before he depart from observing the law." Yet Robertson notes Peter's obstinacy: "Peter had been reared from childhood to make the distinction... and this new proposal even from the Lord runs against all his previous training... He is in spiritual and intellectual turmoil."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter refused — "Not so, Lord; I have never eaten anything common or unclean" — a polite refusal with a reason (Robertson), strikingly in character with his impulsive resistance elsewhere (Ellicott, cf. Matthew 16:22). He pleaded his lifelong observance of the ceremonial law (Leviticus 11, Barnes), not daring to break what God had commanded. Calvin notes the apparent conflict between the law and the vision, which Peter would not resolve hastily; Robertson notes his obstinacy and turmoil, reared from childhood in the distinction and now confronted with a command that overturned it. The verse displays the depth of Peter's scruple: even a voice from heaven could not at once overcome his lifelong training. The conflict in his mind — between the old law and the new command — is the very thing the vision was given to resolve. The verse marks the struggle of the apostle to receive the new truth: the breaking of so deep a prejudice required a threefold repetition (v.16) and the confirming events that followed. Peter's "Not so, Lord" is the honest resistance of a conscience bound by the old order, soon to be set free.
15:
"And the voice spake unto him again the second time, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common." The witnesses note the divine correction of Peter's scruple and the deep meaning of the vision. On the immediate sense, Barnes notes it removed the clean/unclean distinction in food; but the higher design was greater: "It was to show him that they who had been esteemed by the Jews as unclean or profane — the entire Gentile world — might now be admitted to similar privileges with the Jews. That barrier was to be broken down (Ephesians 2:14; Galatians 3:28)."

The grammatical voices note the force of the command. Robertson: "Do thou stop making common what God cleansed... Peter had just called common what God had invited him to slay and eat." Barnes draws the legal maxim: when the reason for a law ceases, the law becomes obsolete — so the ceremonial laws, made to keep Jew and Gentile separate, passed away when the separation was abolished.

The Reformed Calvin extends the principle to all of life and against human prohibitions: "It is not for us to allow or condemn any thing; but as we stand and fall by the judgment of God alone, so is he judge of all things." He applies it against the Papist food laws and all who forbid what God allows: "if there start up a mortal man, making a new difference, forbidding certain meats, he taketh unto himself the authority and power of God by sacrilegious boldness."

The witnesses agree: the voice corrected Peter — "What God hath cleansed, call not thou common." The immediate sense removed the clean/unclean distinction in food, but the higher design (Barnes) was to teach that the Gentiles, esteemed unclean, might now be admitted to the same privileges as the Jews — the middle wall broken (Ephesians 2:14). Robertson notes the force: "stop making common what God cleansed." Barnes draws the maxim that ceremonial laws, made to separate Jew and Gentile, passed away when the separation ceased. Calvin extends the principle against all human prohibitions of what God allows (the Papist food laws), since God alone is judge. The verse displays the divine abolition of the old barrier: what God has cleansed — the Gentiles, the formerly forbidden — Peter may no longer call common. The verse is the doctrinal heart of the chapter: the breaking down of the wall between Jew and Gentile, the opening of the church to all nations. The vision, given to overturn Peter's scruple, teaches the church in every age that God's cleansing, not human tradition, determines what is clean — and that none whom God receives may be despised as common or unclean.
16:
"This was done thrice: and the vessel was received up again into heaven." The witnesses note the threefold repetition and its purpose. On the "thrice," the grammatical voices note it was for confirmation. Barnes: "Three times, doubtless, to impress the mind of Peter with the certainty and importance of the vision" (cf. Genesis 41:32). Ellicott connects it to the threefold "Feed my sheep" (John 21:15-17): "mystically the token of a complete ratification of the truth proclaimed."

On Peter's slowness, Robertson notes the threefold repetition was needed because "Peter remained unconvinced even by the prohibition of God. Here is a striking illustration of obstinacy on the part of one who acknowledges the voice of God to him when the command of the Lord crosses one's preferences and prejudices." The depth of his scruple required the repetition.

The Reformed Calvin notes the confirmation and Peter's lingering perplexity: "The repetition of the vision served for the confirmation of Peter, lest any doubt should remain in his mind. Whence we gather how deeply the observing of the law was rooted in his mind." The vessel taken up into heaven certified that the message came from God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vision was repeated thrice and the sheet taken up into heaven — the threefold repetition for confirmation, to impress its certainty and importance (Barnes, cf. Genesis 41:32), perhaps echoing the threefold "Feed my sheep" (Ellicott). The repetition was needed because of Peter's deep-rooted scruple and obstinacy, unconvinced even by God's prohibition until thrice confirmed (Robertson, Calvin). The sheet's return to heaven certified that the message came from God. The verse displays both the certainty of the revelation and the slowness of Peter to receive it: so deeply was the ceremonial law rooted in his mind that God repeated the vision three times. The verse marks the completion of the symbolic revelation: the lesson confirmed, the sheet withdrawn, Peter left to ponder its meaning (v.17) until the arrival of the messengers (vv.17-18) would interpret it. The threefold vision is God's emphatic teaching of a truth Peter found hard to receive — that the old distinction is abolished, and the Gentiles are to be received. The depth of the prejudice measures the greatness of the change God was working in the apostle and the church.
17:
"Now while Peter doubted in himself what this vision which he had seen should mean, behold, the men which were sent from Cornelius had made enquiry for Simon's house, and stood before the gate," The witnesses note the providential timing of the messengers' arrival as Peter pondered. The grammatical voices note Peter's perplexity. Robertson: "When out of the ecstasy he was more puzzled than ever." The vision's full meaning was not yet clear to him.

On the providential coincidence, Barnes draws the lesson: "We see here an admirable arrangement of the events of Providence to fit each other... just in the moment when the mind of Peter was filled with perplexity, the very event should occur which should relieve him of his embarrassment." Clarke marvels at the exact convergence: "How exactly does every thing in the conduct of Providence occur; and how completely is every thing adapted to time, place, and occasion!"

Clarke also draws a caution against misreading providence: "Those simple occurrences which men snatch at, and press into the service of their own wishes, and call them providential openings, may, indeed, be links of a providential chain... but unless they be found to speak the same language in all their parts... they are not to be construed as indications of the Divine will." Here, vision and event corresponded exactly.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as Peter pondered the vision's meaning (more puzzled than ever after the trance, Robertson), the messengers from Cornelius arrived at the gate — the providential arrangement of events to fit each other, the very event arriving to relieve his perplexity (Barnes). Clarke marvels at the exact convergence of time, place, and occasion, and draws a caution against misreading providence: only when vision and event correspond in all their parts (as here) are they a sure indication of God's will. The verse displays the converging providence: Peter's vision and Cornelius's messengers, prepared independently, meet at the precise moment. The apostle, perplexed by the vision, is about to have its meaning unlocked by the arrival of the Gentile messengers — the event interpreting the symbol. The verse marks the meeting of the two halves of God's plan: the vision that prepared Peter and the messengers that called him, converging to open the door to the Gentiles. The God who orchestrates events to fit his purpose brings the puzzled apostle and the seeking Gentiles together at the appointed gate and hour.
18:
"And called, and asked whether Simon, which was surnamed Peter, were lodged there." The brief verse notes the messengers' inquiry. The grammatical voices note the calling and asking. Robertson: "Called. In a loud voice that those inside the house might hear. Asked... Kept on inquiring." The messengers, having found the house, called out and inquired for Peter.

The inquiry the witnesses read as the completion of the messengers' errand. Having traveled from Caesarea and made diligent inquiry to find the obscure tanner's house (Robertson, under v.17), they now call at the gate, asking for Peter by name — the name the angel had given Cornelius (v.5).

The verse marks the moment of contact between the Gentile messengers and the household where Peter lodged. The providence that brought them precisely as Peter pondered the vision (v.17) now brings them to the gate, calling for the apostle. The Spirit would immediately direct Peter to them (vv.19-20).

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the messengers called out and inquired whether Peter lodged there (Robertson) — completing their errand, having found the obscure tanner's house after diligent inquiry, and asking for Peter by the very name the angel had given Cornelius (v.5). The verse marks the moment of contact: the Gentile messengers at the gate, calling for the apostle, precisely as he pondered the vision (v.17). The verse, brief as it is, is the hinge of the converging providence: the messengers' inquiry, heard within the house, would prompt the Spirit's word to Peter (vv.19-20) directing him to go with them. The God who arranged the two visions now brings the messengers to the very gate, that the apostle and the Gentiles' envoys may meet. The calling at the gate is the answer to Peter's perplexity (v.17): the event that interprets the vision is now at hand. The Gentile messengers, seeking Peter, are about to receive him — and through him, the gospel will come to Cornelius and the Gentile world. The verse, simple as it is, displays the exactness of the converging providence: the messengers do not wander or delay, but call at the very house and gate where the apostle lodges, asking for him by name. The God who prepared both the vision and the messengers now brings them together at the precise moment, that nothing of his design might fail. The seeking of the Gentiles and the readiness of the apostle meet at the threshold, and the great work of opening the door to the nations is about to begin.
19:
"While Peter thought on the vision, the Spirit said unto him, Behold, three men seek thee." The witnesses note the Spirit's direct word to Peter as he pondered. The puritan Henry and the witnesses note the convergence of inner and outer guidance. Wesley (under v.17): "Frequently the things which befall us within and from without at the same time, are a key to each other." The vision within and the messengers without, together, unlock God's will.

On "the Spirit said," the grammatical voices note the direct guidance of the Holy Spirit. Robertson (under v.19): the Spirit directs Peter to the men, connecting their mission with the vision still troubling him. The Spirit's word resolves Peter's perplexity by directing him to act.

The witnesses note the personal, immediate character of the Spirit's guidance. As Peter still thought on the vision, the Spirit spoke, announcing the three men — the interpretation of the vision now at hand. The Spirit's direction prepares Peter to go with the Gentile messengers without scruple (v.20).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: as Peter pondered the vision, the Spirit said directly, "Behold, three men seek thee" — the convergence of inner vision and outer event, together the key to God's will (Wesley). The Spirit's word resolves Peter's perplexity by directing him to the men, connecting their mission with the vision (Robertson). The verse displays the direct, personal guidance of the Holy Spirit: as the apostle thought on the heavenly symbol, the Spirit spoke, announcing the messengers and so unlocking the meaning of the vision. The verse marks the third element of the converging providence: the vision (prepared Peter), the messengers (called him), and now the Spirit (directs him to go). The God who orchestrates the meeting leaves nothing to chance: the Spirit himself announces the men and bids Peter go. The verse displays the harmony of God's guidance — vision, event, and inward word agreeing — by which the apostle is led, step by step, to the house of Cornelius and the opening of the door to the Gentiles. The Spirit who fell at Pentecost now directs the gospel's advance to the Gentile world, speaking to Peter at the very moment of his perplexity.
20:
"Arise therefore, and get thee down, and go with them, doubting nothing: for I have sent them." The witnesses note the Spirit's command to go without doubt, and his ownership of the mission. On "doubting nothing," the grammatical voices note the verb (wavering, a divided mind). Robertson: "Nothing doubting... the notion of wavering is common with this verb." Ellicott: "For a time he was to walk, as it were, blindfold, but trusting in the full assurance of faith in the Hand that was guiding him."

On "for I have sent them," Robertson notes: "The Holy Spirit assumes responsibility for the messengers from Cornelius and thus connects their mission with the vision." The Spirit himself owns the sending of the Gentile messengers — the surest warrant for Peter to go.

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson on the obedience of faith: "we must not follow God with a doubtful and wavering, but with a quiet and constant mind... when we hear him we dispute no longer what we have need to do, but that we set down for a certainty that that must be done which he commandeth." The reason ("I have sent them") shows that God's will, once known, ends all disputation.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Spirit commanded Peter to go with the messengers "doubting nothing" — without wavering or a divided mind (Robertson), walking by faith though not yet seeing the full purpose (Ellicott) — "for I have sent them," the Spirit himself owning the mission. Calvin draws the lesson of the obedience of faith: God's will, once known, ends all disputation; we must follow him with a quiet and constant mind. The verse displays the divine warrant for Peter's great step: the Spirit, who sent the messengers, bids the apostle go without doubt. The command overcomes Peter's lingering scruple (the vision had prepared him; the Spirit now commands him). The verse marks the decisive moment when Peter, directed by the Spirit, prepares to step over the line of Jewish custom and go to the Gentiles. The "I have sent them" is the seal of God's purpose: the meeting with Cornelius is no human arrangement but the Spirit's own design. Peter, bidden to doubt nothing, is to obey in faith — and his obedience opens the door of the gospel to the Gentile world. The Spirit's command turns the puzzled apostle into the willing instrument of God's universal grace.
21:
"Then Peter went down to the men which were sent unto him from Cornelius; and said, Behold, I am he whom ye seek: what is the cause wherefore ye are come?" The witnesses note Peter's prompt obedience and his inquiry. The grammatical voices note his readiness. Clarke: "being informed by the Holy Spirit that three men were seeking him, and that he should go with them without scruple, he instantly obeyed; and finding them at the door, desired to know why they sought him."

The Reformed Calvin notes that Peter now understood the vision's meaning through the messengers: "he heareth that he is called by Cornelius, a man that is a Gentile, whom he would have counted profane... unless his judgment had been corrected with this voice, What God calleth pure, judge not thou to be common. This is to be wise indeed, when as abandoning all vain confidence... the authority of God doth so pull us unto it."

Peter's inquiry the witnesses read as his readiness to learn the purpose of the call. Though directed by the Spirit, he asks the messengers their errand — that he and those with him might know the occasion fully, and that the providence might be plainly declared.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter, directed by the Spirit, went down at once to the messengers and asked their errand — his prompt obedience showing he now understood the vision through the call from a Gentile (Clarke, Calvin). Calvin notes the wisdom of submitting to God's authority, abandoning the old prejudice: the vision ("call not thou common") had corrected Peter's judgment, so that he would go to a Gentile he would once have counted profane. The verse displays the transformed apostle: the man who had refused the unclean (v.14) now goes willingly to meet the Gentiles' messengers, the vision's meaning unlocked. His inquiry shows his readiness to learn the purpose of the call. The verse marks Peter's first step over the line of Jewish custom: obeying the Spirit, he meets the Gentile envoys and asks their errand. The providence that prepared him by vision and directed him by the Spirit now moves him to action — the apostle ready, the door about to open. Peter, lately scrupulous, is now the willing instrument of the gospel's advance to the Gentiles, submitting his judgment wholly to the authority of God.
22:
"And they said, Cornelius the centurion, a just man, and one that feareth God, and of good report among all the nation of the Jews, was warned from God by an holy angel to send for thee into his house, and to hear words of thee." The witnesses note the messengers' commendation of Cornelius. The grammatical voices note the threefold description: just (righteous in the Jewish sense), God-fearing (the God-fearer/proselyte of the gate), and of good report among all the Jews. Robertson: "It is the believing ones... a God-fearing proselyte."

On the good report among the Jews, Ellicott notes Luke's policy of conciliation: "all occasion of offence was, as far as possible, guarded against by the attestation given by those who were themselves Jews to the character of those concerned." Ellicott also recalls the centurion of Capernaum (Luke 7:5) and Christ's word about many coming from east and west.

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose of the commendation: "Cornelius' servants commend their master not ambitiously... but that Peter may the less abhor his company... they say that he was approved of the Jews, that Peter may know that he was not estranged from true and sincere godliness." And they stress the divine origin: he was "warned from God by an holy angel" — the surest warrant for Peter.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the messengers commended Cornelius as a just, God-fearing man of good report among all the Jews, warned by an angel to send for Peter. The threefold description marks him as a righteous God-fearer (Robertson); and Luke's stress on his good report among the Jews is the policy of conciliation, guarding against offence by Jewish attestation of his character (Ellicott). Calvin notes the purpose: that Peter might the less abhor the company of a Gentile so approved, and might know the call was of divine origin (the angel). The verse displays the providential preparation: the Gentile to whom Peter is sent is no profligate pagan but a man of sincere piety, attested even by the Jews, called by God himself. The commendation removes every obstacle to Peter's coming. The verse marks the convergence of all the warrants: the vision, the Spirit's command, and now the testimony of Cornelius's character and divine call. The God-fearing centurion, righteous and well-reported, awaits the apostle to "hear words" of him — the gospel that would open to him and to the Gentile world the door of faith and salvation.
23:
"Then called he them in, and lodged them. And on the morrow Peter went away with them, and certain brethren from Joppa accompanied him." The witnesses note Peter's hospitality and the six brethren who went with him. Chrysostom: "Good, that first he gives the men friendly treatment, after the fatigue of their journey, and makes them at home with him." Peter, having received the Spirit's command, lodges the Gentile messengers — itself a step over the old barrier.

On the brethren, the grammatical voices note they were six (11:12). Clarke: "It was necessary that there should be several witnesses of the important transactions which were about to take place; as on no slight evidence would even the converted Jews believe that repentance unto life, and the Holy Spirit, should be granted to the Gentiles." Robertson: "The wisdom of having these half dozen Jewish Christians... becomes manifest in Jerusalem (11:12)."

The taking of the brethren the witnesses read as providential. Ellicott: "that in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word might be established (Deuteronomy 17:6)... that they might report to the Church at Joppa what had been done by the Apostle." The six witnesses would vindicate Peter's action before the church at Jerusalem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter lodged the messengers (showing them hospitality after their journey, Chrysostom — itself a step over the old barrier) and went with them the next day, accompanied by six brethren from Joppa (11:12). The taking of the brethren was providential: as witnesses, that the great transaction — the granting of repentance and the Spirit to the Gentiles — might be established on multiple testimony (Clarke, Ellicott, Deuteronomy 17:6), and might vindicate Peter's action before the church at Jerusalem (Robertson, 11:12). The verse displays both Peter's growing freedom from the old scruple (lodging Gentiles) and the divine wisdom in providing witnesses. The apostle, obedient to the Spirit, sets out for Caesarea with the Gentile messengers and Jewish brethren — the company that would witness the opening of the door to the Gentiles. The verse marks the journey toward the epochal meeting: Peter, no longer doubting, goes to Cornelius, taking witnesses who would later confirm to the skeptical church that God had indeed received the Gentiles. The providence that prepared the meeting also provides for its vindication — that the great change might be established beyond dispute.
24:
"And the morrow after they entered into Caesarea. And Cornelius waited for them, and had called together his kinsmen and near friends." The witnesses note Cornelius's eager expectation and his gathering of others. The grammatical voices note his waiting. Robertson: "in eager expectation and hope." Wesley: "Not engaging himself in any secular business during that solemn time, but being altogether intent on this one thing."

On the gathering of kinsmen and friends, the Reformed Calvin draws out Cornelius's love for others' salvation: "he considered with himself how much he was indebted both to the glory of God, and also to the salvation of his brethren. He knew that it was an unjust thing... to provide for himself alone... Cornelius hath taught us by his example, that when God revealeth himself unto us we must not choke the light of his knowledge with sloth or fear, but we must rather endeavor that our faith may shine before others."

Calvin also notes Cornelius's humility in gathering them as fellow-learners: "he is far from vainglory; for he calleth them to be his school-fellows, being himself ready to learn." The verse displays a man who, expecting a blessing, would have his kinsmen and friends share it.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius waited eagerly for Peter (intent on this one thing, setting aside all business, Wesley, Robertson) and had gathered his kinsmen and near friends. Calvin draws out his love for others' salvation: knowing he owed God's glory and his brethren's good, he would not keep the blessing to himself but gathered others to share it — and in humility, as fellow-learners, himself ready to learn. The verse displays the godly character of Cornelius: eager for the word, and eager that his kinsmen and friends should hear it too. His gathering of others is the model of one who, finding light, would share it; who, expecting a blessing, would have his household and friends partake. The verse marks the assembling of the first Gentile congregation to hear the gospel: Cornelius, his kinsmen, and his friends, waiting in expectation for Peter. The providence that prepared the apostle and the centurion now brings together the company to whom the gospel would be preached and on whom the Spirit would fall (v.44). The devout centurion's love for his friends' salvation makes him gather them to hear — and so the door opens not to one man but to a household and a circle of Gentiles.
25:
"And as Peter was coming in, Cornelius met him, and fell down at his feet, and worshipped him." The witnesses note Cornelius's act of homage and prepare for Peter's refusal. The grammatical voices note the nature of the act. Barnes: "an act of profound regard for him as an ambassador of God... It does not mean religious homage, but civil respect." Robertson: "reverence like old English usage, and not actual worship, though Peter took it that way."

The Reformed Calvin examines the act carefully and reads it as an excess that touched on the divine: "I think that he meant nothing less than to spoil God of his lawful worship, that he might give it to man; but forasmuch as he meant to give singular honor unto the prophet and apostle of Christ, he fell into an immoderate token of reverence, and so he offended in excess." He notes how prone men are to superstition when honour with any show of divine worship is given to Christ's ministers.

Calvin uses this against the Papist distinctions (latria, dulia, hyperdulia) and the worship of saints: "no worship whereunto religion is annexed... doth leave to God his honor untouched, what man soever it have; therefore Peter is content with this one reason, that he is a man." The same lesson appears in John's worship of the angel (Revelation 22:8-9).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius met Peter and fell at his feet in homage — civil respect rather than religious worship in intent (Barnes, Robertson), yet an excess that touched on the divine, which Peter would refuse (v.26). Calvin reads it as an immoderate reverence offered to an apostle, into which Cornelius fell from excess of honour — and uses it against the Papist worship of saints and the distinctions of latria and dulia: no worship with religion annexed leaves God his honour untouched, so Peter rests on the one reason, "I am a man." The verse displays the danger of excessive reverence to Christ's ministers: even a sincere man, in honouring the apostle, fell into an act that bordered on the worship due to God alone. The verse sets up Peter's refusal (v.26), a standing rebuke to all undue veneration of men — and, the witnesses note, a witness against the cult of saints and the claims made for Peter's supposed successors. The honour due to God alone may not be given to any man, however holy; and the apostle himself refused what his successors are said to receive.
26:
"But Peter took him up, saying, Stand up; I myself also am a man." The witnesses note Peter's refusal of homage and its abiding lesson. Chrysostom: "Do you mark how, before all else, the Apostles teach them this lesson, not to think great things of them?" Peter at once lifts Cornelius up and disclaims any honour above a man.

On the lesson, Ellicott draws the contrast with later practice: "Peter's answer, in strong contrast with the words and acts, the very ceremonial, of those who claim to be his successors, shows that he looked on it as expressing a homage such as God alone could rightly claim." He compares the angel's words in Revelation 22:9 ("worship God") and Paul at Lystra ("We also are men of like passions," Acts 14:15).

The witnesses note the apostolic humility. Wesley: "I myself also am a man — And not God, who alone ought to be worshipped (Matthew 4:10). Have all his pretended successors attended to this?" Barnes: Peter practically said, "I am ready in civil life to show thee all the respect that is due, but I am nothing more than a man."

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter lifted Cornelius up and refused the homage — "Stand up; I myself also am a man." The apostles teach, before all else, not to think great things of them (Chrysostom). The refusal is a standing rebuke to all undue veneration of men, in strong contrast with the ceremonial claimed by Peter's supposed successors (Ellicott, Wesley) — paralleling the angel's "worship God" (Revelation 22:9) and Paul's "we also are men" (Acts 14:15). The verse displays apostolic humility: Peter, the chief of the apostles, claims no honour above a man and will not receive what borders on the worship due to God alone. The verse is a witness against the cult of saints and the exaltation of Peter himself: the apostle refused the very homage that, the witnesses note, his alleged successors receive. The lesson is plain — no man, however holy or honoured, may receive the worship due to God; and the truest greatness is the humility that disclaims it. Peter's "I myself also am a man" is the model of the minister who points men away from himself to God, and the perpetual rebuke of all who would be worshipped or who worship the creature in place of the Creator.
27:
"And as he talked with him, he went in, and found many that were come together." The brief verse notes Peter's entrance and the company assembled. The grammatical voices note the conversation. Robertson: "συνομιλεω, rare compound... as he talked with him." Ellicott: "The word implies a conversation of some length; possibly leading to the resolve that each should state separately how they had been brought together."

On the company, the witnesses note the assembly of Gentiles Cornelius had gathered (v.24). Robertson: "It was an expectant group of Gentiles eager for Peter's interpretation of the vision of Cornelius." Peter, entering the Gentile house, found a congregation waiting to hear the word.

The entrance the witnesses read as Peter's decisive step over the old barrier. The apostle, who once would not keep company with a Gentile (v.28), now enters Cornelius's house and finds the assembled company — the breaking of the wall in action. The conversation prepared the way for the mutual statement of how God had brought them together.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Peter, talking with Cornelius, entered the house and found the many Gentiles assembled — an expectant group eager for his word (Robertson). The conversation, of some length, may have led to the resolve that each should state how they were brought together (Ellicott). The verse displays Peter's decisive step over the old barrier: the apostle, who by Jewish custom would not enter a Gentile's house (v.28), now goes in and finds the waiting congregation — the breaking of the wall in action. The verse marks the meeting of Peter and the first Gentile congregation: the apostle within the Gentile house, the company assembled to hear the gospel. The providence that prepared the meeting now brings the apostle face to face with the eager Gentiles. The verse, brief as it is, is a milestone: a Jewish apostle, in a Gentile home, about to preach Christ to the uncircumcised — the door of faith opening before the assembled company. Peter's entrance is itself the visible proof that the old separation is abolished and that God has cleansed what was once called common.
28:
"And he said unto them, Ye know how that it is an unlawful thing for a man that is a Jew to keep company, or come unto one of another nation; but God hath shewed me that I should not call any man common or unclean." The witnesses note Peter's explanation of the barrier and its abolition. The grammatical voices note the source of the prohibition. Robertson: "there is no O.T. regulation forbidding such social contact with Gentiles, though the rabbis had added it and had made it binding by custom." Ellicott: "St. Peter speaks from the standpoint of traditional Pharisaism rather than from that of the Law itself."

On the breaking of the wall, Ellicott draws the deep meaning: "Humanity as such had been redeemed by the Incarnation and Ascension, and was no longer common or unclean, even in the most outcast heathen... men were taught to see even in the sinner the potentialities of a higher life." He connects it to "Honour all men" (1 Peter 2:17). Robertson: "It is that middle wall of partition between Jew and Gentile (Ephesians 2:14) which Jesus broke down."

The Reformed Calvin notes that the prohibition came from custom, not the law itself, and that Peter now declares the wall pulled down: "because the wall of separation was pulled down, and the covenant of life is now common to them both alike, he saith that those are not to be counted aliens who are made partakers of God's adoption."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter explained the barrier — the Jews held it unlawful to keep company with Gentiles (a rabbinic custom, not the law itself, Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin) — and declared its abolition: "God hath shewed me that I should not call any man common or unclean." This is the application of the vision (v.15): humanity itself, redeemed by the Incarnation and Ascension, is no longer common or unclean (Ellicott, cf. "Honour all men," 1 Peter 2:17); the middle wall (Ephesians 2:14) is broken. The verse displays the doctrinal fruit of the vision: Peter now sees that no man is to be despised as common, the old separation between Jew and Gentile abolished. The verse marks the apostle's open confession of the new truth before the Gentile congregation: God has taught him to call no man unclean. The vision that overturned his scruple about food has overturned his scruple about men; and the gospel is now to be offered freely to the Gentiles. Peter's words are the charter of the church's universality: in Christ, the barriers of nation and ceremony are removed, and every man, however outcast, is to be honoured as one whom God may cleanse and receive.
29:
"Therefore came I unto you without gainsaying, as soon as I was sent for: I ask therefore for what intent ye have sent for me?" The witnesses note Peter's prompt obedience and his inquiry into the purpose. The grammatical voices note "without gainsaying." Robertson: "Without answering back... That is true after the Holy Spirit expressly told Peter to go." Peter came without objection, his earlier scruples overcome.

On the inquiry, the witnesses note Peter's reason for asking. Wesley: "St. Peter knew this already. But he puts Cornelius on telling the story, both that the rest might be informed, and Cornelius himself more impressed by the narration." Clarke: "that his friends might be the better prepared to receive the truth."

The Reformed Calvin draws the lesson of faith's silence: "This is the holy silence of faith, when as without murmuring against God we receive that meekly which he commandeth, abandoning all contrary reasons which intrude themselves." Peter's coming "without gainsaying" is the model of obedient faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter came without objection (his scruples overcome by the Spirit's command, Robertson) and asked the purpose of the call — though he knew it (v.22), that Cornelius might tell the story for the benefit of the company and be the more impressed (Wesley, Clarke). Calvin draws the lesson of faith's holy silence: receiving God's command meekly without murmuring. The verse displays the obedient apostle: come without gainsaying, ready to learn the occasion fully, that the providence might be plainly declared before all. Peter's inquiry, though he knew the answer, draws out Cornelius's testimony (vv.30-33), so that the whole company might understand how God had brought them together. The verse marks the model of obedient faith ("without gainsaying") and the wisdom of letting the divine providence be openly told. The apostle, once scrupulous, now stands willingly in the Gentile house, asking the purpose of his call — and the answer (Cornelius's recap of his vision) would confirm to all that the meeting was of God. Faith's silence and obedience open the way for the gospel; and Peter's readiness is the fruit of the lesson the vision taught.
30:
"And Cornelius said, Four days ago I was fasting until this hour; and at the ninth hour I prayed in my house, and, behold, a man stood before me in bright clothing," The witnesses note Cornelius's recounting of his vision. The grammatical voices note the timing. Robertson: "Four days ago, reckoning backwards." Ellicott reconstructs the timeline: Cornelius's vision on one day, Peter's on the next, the meeting on the Sabbath, the outpouring of the Spirit perhaps at the beginning of the first day of the week.

On the fasting, the witnesses note its connection with his prayer. Calvin: "he maketh express mention of fasting, partly that we may know that he prayed not coldly... secondly, that the vision may be the less suspected. For doubtless the brain of a man that is fasting... doth not easily admit any strong imaginations." The fasting marked the earnestness of his prayer and guarded against the charge of fantasy.

On "a man in bright clothing," the witnesses note the angel appearing as a man in shining raiment (the token of heavenly glory, Calvin), as at the resurrection (Matthew 28:3). Cornelius modestly calls him "a man," though he knew him an angel (Chrysostom, under v.23).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Cornelius recounted his vision — fasting and praying at the ninth hour, an angel ("a man in bright clothing") appeared. The fasting marked the earnestness of his prayer and guarded the vision against the charge of fantasy (Calvin); the bright clothing was the token of heavenly glory (as at the resurrection, Matthew 28:3); and Cornelius modestly calls the angel "a man." The verse displays the devout centurion's recounting of God's dealing with him: a man of prayer and fasting, to whom God sent an angel in answer. The verse marks the beginning of Cornelius's testimony (vv.30-33), by which the whole company would learn how God had brought them together. The repetition of the vision, now from Cornelius's lips, confirms to all that the meeting was of divine arrangement. The fasting and prayer of Cornelius are the setting in which God revealed himself — the devout seeker met by the messenger of God. The verse underscores that the conversion of the Gentiles began not in human initiative but in God's answer to a praying, fasting, God-fearing man, whom he directed to the apostle for the fuller light of the gospel.
31:
"And said, Cornelius, thy prayer is heard, and thine alms are had in remembrance in the sight of God." The witnesses note the angel's assurance, repeated from v.4. The grammatical voices note the singular "thy prayer." Ellicott: "The singular number gives a greater definiteness to the object of the prayer than in Acts 10:4. It must have been a prayer for fuller light and knowledge of the Truth." Cornelius had prayed for guidance, and his prayer was heard.

On the prayer and alms remembered, Clarke draws the lesson of improving grace: "Cornelius prayed, fasted, and gave alms. It was in this way he looked for salvation; not to purchase it... He that hath, i.e., that uses what he has, shall receive; and no man can expect any increase of light or life, who does not improve the grace already given." Wesley: "he had been praying for instruction, how to worship God in the most acceptable manner."

The repetition of the assurance the witnesses read as the confirmation of God's acceptance. The angel's words, now recounted by Cornelius, declare again that his prayers and alms were remembered before God — and answered with the sending of Peter. The devout seeker's prayer for light is granted.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the angel assured Cornelius that his prayer was heard and his alms remembered before God (repeated from v.4). The singular "thy prayer" points to a definite request — for fuller light and knowledge of the truth (Ellicott); Cornelius had prayed for guidance how to worship God acceptably (Wesley), and his prayer was answered with the sending of Peter. Clarke draws the lesson of improving grace: Cornelius used the light he had (prayer, fasting, alms), and God increased it; none can expect more light who does not improve what is given. The verse displays God's acceptance and answer of the devout seeker: his prayers and alms, wrought by grace, were remembered, and his prayer for light was granted in the coming of the apostle. The verse, recounting the angel's assurance, confirms to the company that God had heard Cornelius and arranged the meeting. The devout centurion's seeking was met by God's answering: he that uses the grace he has receives more. The verse teaches that God remembers and answers the prayers of those who seek him sincerely — and crowns their seeking with the fuller knowledge of Christ through the preaching of the gospel.
32:
"Send therefore to Joppa, and call hither Simon, whose surname is Peter; he is lodged in the house of one Simon a tanner by the sea side: who, when he cometh, shall speak unto thee." The brief verse repeats the angel's direction. The grammatical voices note the repetition of the precise instruction (Robertson). Cornelius recounts the angel's exact words: send to Joppa for Simon Peter at the tanner's house.

The repetition the witnesses read as confirming the divine arrangement. The same precise direction given in vv.5-6 is now recounted by Cornelius, establishing before the company that the meeting was ordered by God, down to the name and place. The angel's promise — "he shall speak unto thee" — points to the purpose: the gospel Peter would preach.

The verse marks the completion of Cornelius's account of the divine command. Having recounted the vision (vv.30-31) and the direction (v.32), he comes to his prompt obedience (v.33). The repetition underscores the exactness and certainty of God's leading.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: Cornelius recounted the angel's precise direction — send to Joppa for Simon Peter at the tanner's house, "who shall speak unto thee" (Robertson). The repetition of the exact instruction (from vv.5-6) confirms before the company that the meeting was ordered by God, down to the name and place. The angel's promise — "he shall speak unto thee" — points to the purpose: the gospel Peter would preach. The verse marks the completion of Cornelius's account of the divine command: the vision, the assurance, and the direction all recounted, establishing that the meeting was no human contrivance but God's own arrangement. The exactness of the direction (the name, the host, the place) is the seal of divine providence. The verse, brief as it is, underscores the certainty of God's leading: the devout centurion was directed by name to the very apostle through whom the gospel would come to the Gentiles. The repetition prepares for Cornelius's profession of readiness to hear (v.33): the company, having heard how God arranged the meeting, is now prepared to receive the word as from God himself. The providence is confirmed; the stage is set for the sermon.
33:
"Immediately therefore I sent to thee; and thou hast well done that thou art come. Now therefore are we all here present before God, to hear all things that are commanded thee of God." The witnesses note Cornelius's obedience, gratitude, and readiness to hear. On "thou hast well done," the grammatical voices note the idiom of heartfelt thanks. Robertson: "A regular formula for expressing thanks (Philippians 4:14). Cornelius commends Peter for his courage in breaking away from Jewish custom."

On "we are all here present before God," the Reformed Calvin draws out the right disposition of hearers: "so often as the Word of God is set before us, we must thus think with ourselves, that we have not to deal with a mortal man, but that God is present, and doth call us. For, from this respect of God ariseth the majesty of God's word, and reverence in hearing the same." Cornelius and his company stand before God to hear, ready to obey all that God commands.

Calvin notes the perfect readiness of faith: "This only is true faith when we embrace not the one half of the Word of God alone, but addict ourselves wholly unto it... Cornelius doth wisely distinguish between God and man, for he maketh God the author of the doctrine, and leaveth nothing for man besides the ministry." The hearers are set to receive Peter's words as God's own.

The thin comment matches the verse.
