The witnesses agree: Cornelius, having obeyed immediately, thanks Peter for coming (the idiom of heartfelt gratitude, commending his courage in breaking Jewish custom, Robertson) and declares the company present "before God, to hear all things commanded thee of God." Calvin draws out the right disposition of hearers: they receive the word not as a mortal man's but as God's, standing before him in reverence, ready to obey all he commands — the perfect readiness of faith that embraces the whole word. The verse displays the ideal congregation: assembled before God, eager, reverent, ready to receive and obey the gospel. Cornelius rightly distinguishes God (the author of the doctrine) from man (the minister), leaving Peter only the ministry. The verse marks the prepared soil for the gospel: a company of God-fearing Gentiles, gathered in expectation, treating the preacher's word as God's own. The verse sets the stage for Peter's sermon (vv.34-43): hearers so disposed are ready for the seed of the word and the gift of the Spirit (v.44). Cornelius's readiness — "to hear all things commanded thee of God" — is the model of the heart prepared to receive the gospel and to obey it wholly.
34:
"Then Peter opened his mouth, and said, Of a truth I perceive that God is no respecter of persons:" The witnesses note the great truth with which Peter opens his sermon. The puritan Henry notes the solemnity of "opened his mouth": he spoke with gravity and freedom on this great occasion. The grammatical voices note Peter's new perception. The vision and the events had taught him a truth he had not before grasped.

On "no respecter of persons," the witnesses note the abolition of national privilege. Barnes: "acceptance with God does not depend on the fact of being descended from Abraham, or of possessing external privileges, but on the state of the heart." Robertson: "a Gentile would not have to become a Jew in order to become a Christian. Evidently Peter had not before perceived this fact." Christ can save Gentiles like Cornelius without their becoming Jews.

The phrase the witnesses connect to Paul's teaching (Romans 2:11) and the whole gospel. God does not regard the outward distinctions of nation or rank but the heart; and so the door of the gospel is open to all peoples alike. Peter's new perception is the doctrinal foundation of the Gentile mission.

The thin comment matches the verse, which opens the sermon. The witnesses agree: Peter opened his mouth with solemnity (Henry) and declared the great truth he now perceived — "God is no respecter of persons." Acceptance with God depends not on descent from Abraham or external privilege but on the state of the heart (Barnes); a Gentile need not become a Jew to become a Christian (Robertson) — a truth Peter had not before grasped. The phrase, echoed by Paul (Romans 2:11), is the doctrinal foundation of the Gentile mission: God regards not nation or rank but the heart, and the gospel is open to all peoples alike. The verse displays the dawning of the great truth in Peter's mind: the vision and the events have taught him that God shows no partiality, and so the Gentiles are to be received. The verse marks the doctrinal heart of the chapter and a turning-point in the history of the church: the apostle's open confession that God is impartial, accepting men of every nation. From this foundation Peter proceeds (v.35) to declare who is accepted, and then (vv.36-43) to preach Christ to the Gentile congregation. The truth that God is no respecter of persons opens the gospel to the whole world and is the charter of the church's universal mission.
35:
"But in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him." The witnesses note the universal scope of God's acceptance — and divide on its meaning. The grammatical voices guard against the moralist misreading. Barnes: "it is not said that Cornelius was accepted on account of his good works. Those works were simply an evidence of true piety in the heart... not a meritorious ground of acceptance." He notes the essential difference from those who depend on morality in Christian lands: Cornelius embraced the Savior when offered; they do not.

Clarke gives the strong qualification against those in gospel lands: "Many irreligious men... quote this verse in their own favor... Let such know that if they had been born where the light of the Gospel had never shone... they might speak in this way; but, as they live under the Gospel of Jesus Christ, God will require that they fear him and work righteousness according to the light afforded by that very Gospel."

The Reformed Calvin denies that the verse teaches justification by works: "it is not the universal righteousness whereby a man is judged just before God, but that honesty and innocency which respecteth men." The first acceptance is altogether free, by grace; the works of the regenerate please God, but not by merit, for they are accepted only through faith, which borrows of Christ what works lack. Wesley: "accepted of him — Through Christ, though he knows him not." Ellicott: such men are "already accepted with God," justified by Christ the true Light (John 1:9), though gaining by conversion a fuller knowledge and higher blessedness.

The witnesses agree on the universal scope: in every nation, he that fears God and works righteousness is accepted — no nation excluded. But they guard the meaning against the moralist/universalist misreading. The works are evidence of heart-piety, not the meritorious ground of acceptance (Barnes); the first acceptance is free, by grace, and the works of the regenerate please God only through faith in Christ (Calvin); the God-fearer is accepted "through Christ, though he knows him not" (Wesley), justified by the true Light (Ellicott). Clarke gives the strong caution: those in gospel lands cannot plead this verse, for God requires of them faith and righteousness according to the gospel's light. The verse displays the equity and love of God, who accepts sincere seekers of every nation — yet by grace through faith (in the Light), not by the merit of works. The great truth opens salvation to all peoples without excluding any, while the witnesses deny that it teaches a salvation by mere morality apart from Christ. The God-fearer is accepted, but accepted in Christ, who is the Saviour of all who fear God and work righteousness — and conversion brings the fuller knowledge and higher blessedness of the gospel.
36:
"The word which God sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace by Jesus Christ: (he is Lord of all:)" The witnesses note the opening of the sermon proper and the great parenthesis. The grammatical voices note the difficult construction (the accusative "word"), variously resolved (Barnes, Robertson). The "word" is the gospel God sent to Israel, the message of peace by Jesus Christ.

On "preaching peace," the witnesses note the reconciliation. Robertson: "Gospelizing peace through Jesus Christ. There is no other way to have real peace between individuals and God, between races and nations, than by Jesus Christ" (cf. Ephesians 2:17). Calvin: peace means "the reconciling of men and God, which hath in it the perfect salvation of the Church."

On "he is Lord of all," the witnesses note the triumphant parenthesis. Robertson: "A triumphant parenthesis that Peter throws in as the reason for his new truth. Jesus Christ is Lord of all, both Jews and Gentiles." Ellicott: it guards against the thought that Jesus was only a prophet — he is "Lord of all, of all men, and of all things." This is the ground of the Gentile mission: Christ, Lord of all, is Savior of all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter begins the gospel message — the word God sent to Israel, "preaching peace by Jesus Christ" — and throws in the triumphant parenthesis, "he is Lord of all." The "peace" is reconciliation between God and men, the sum of the church's salvation (Robertson, Calvin, cf. Ephesians 2:17); and there is no real peace, between man and God or race and race, but by Christ. The parenthesis "Lord of all" is the ground of the Gentile mission: Christ, Lord of both Jew and Gentile (Robertson), and no mere prophet but Lord of all men and things (Ellicott), is therefore the Savior of all. The verse displays the heart of the gospel Peter preaches to the Gentiles: peace with God through Jesus Christ, who is Lord of all. The verse marks the transition from the great truth (vv.34-35) to the gospel history (vv.37-43): having declared God's impartiality, Peter proclaims the message of peace by Christ, Lord of all. The lordship of Christ over all nations is the reason the gospel comes to the Gentiles: he who is Lord of all is the Savior of all who believe. Peter, lately taught that God is no respecter of persons, now preaches the universal Lord and the universal peace — the foundation of the church's mission to every nation.
37:
"That word, I say, ye know, which was published throughout all Judaea, and began from Galilee, after the baptism which John preached;" The witnesses note Peter's appeal to what the Gentiles already knew. The grammatical voices note the different word ("that word/thing," the matter or report, Barnes). Peter assumes Cornelius and his company knew the main facts of Jesus' ministry, which had been published throughout Judea.

On the scope and the starting-point, Robertson notes Peter's outline follows the gospel pattern: "The story began with a skip to Galilee after the baptism just like the Gospel of Mark... This first message of Peter to the Gentiles corresponds in broad outline with Mark's Gospel." Mark heard Peter preach and modeled his Gospel on this outline.

The appeal to what they knew the witnesses read as Peter's wise method: building from the known to the unknown. Clarke: "thus he begins, from what they knew, to teach them what they did not know." The facts of Jesus' ministry, widely published, were the foundation on which Peter would build the gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter appealed to what the Gentiles already knew — the matter published throughout Judea, beginning from Galilee after John's baptism (Barnes). His outline follows the gospel pattern (the skip to Galilee after the baptism, like Mark's Gospel, which was modeled on Peter's preaching, Robertson). The appeal to known facts is Peter's wise method: building from the known to the unknown (Clarke), the widely-published ministry of Jesus the foundation for the gospel he would preach. The verse displays the apostolic method of preaching: starting from the facts of Jesus' life, known even to Gentiles in Judea, and proceeding to their saving meaning. The verse marks the beginning of the gospel history in Peter's sermon: the ministry of Jesus, published throughout the land, beginning in Galilee after John's baptism. Peter assumes his hearers' acquaintance with these facts and builds upon them. The verse, brief as it is, shows the historical foundation of the apostolic gospel — the real life and ministry of Jesus, publicly known — on which the proclamation of his death, resurrection, and saving name (vv.38-43) rests. Peter preaches no myth but a public history, witnessed and known, and now interpreted as the gospel of salvation.
38:
"How God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Ghost and with power: who went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil; for God was with him." The witnesses note the summary of Jesus' ministry. On the anointing, the grammatical voices note it refers to his being set apart as Messiah, endowed with the Spirit and power (Barnes, Robertson, cf. his baptism). "Christ" means "anointed"; Peter proclaims Jesus the Messiah, proved by his works (Clarke).

On "went about doing good," the witnesses dwell on the beautiful summary. Barnes: "Whose main business it was to travel from place to place to do good... This is the simple but sublime record of his life." Robertson: "Beautiful description of Jesus... the term applies to Jesus far more than to any earthly king." This single phrase tells the character of the Savior.

On "healing all oppressed of the devil," the witnesses note the reality of Satan and the devil's work in disease. Robertson: "The reality of the devil is recognized by Peter." On "God was with him," Robertson defends it against the charge of a low Christology: Peter uses lowly terms, speaking sparingly of Christ's majesty to his hearers (Wesley), yet the works prove the divine power.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter summarized Jesus' ministry — God anointed him with the Spirit and power (setting him apart as Messiah, proved by his works, Barnes, Clarke); he "went about doing good and healing all oppressed of the devil" (the beautiful, sublime record of his life, Barnes, Robertson); "God was with him." The phrase "went about doing good" is the simplest and most affecting portrait of the Savior. The healing of those "oppressed of the devil" recognizes the reality of Satan and his work in disease (Robertson). "God was with him," a lowly term, is no low Christology but Peter's sparing speech of Christ's majesty to these hearers (Wesley, Robertson), the works themselves proving the divine power. The verse displays the gospel portrait of Jesus: the anointed Messiah, full of the Spirit and power, who spent his life doing good and healing, with God manifestly with him. The verse marks the heart of the gospel history: the public ministry of Jesus, doing good and conquering the devil's works, attested by God. Peter preaches the real, beneficent ministry of the Savior — the Messiah anointed for the salvation of men — as the foundation of the saving gospel he proclaims to the Gentiles.
39:
"And we are witnesses of all things which he did both in the land of the Jews, and in Jerusalem; whom they slew and hanged on a tree." The witnesses note the apostolic testimony and the crucifixion. The grammatical voices note Peter's appeal to eyewitness testimony. Robertson: "Peter thus appeals to what the audience know and to what the disciples know. He made the same claim about personal witnesses... at Pentecost (2:32)." The apostles were eyewitnesses of Jesus' works.

On "whom they slew and hanged on a tree," the witnesses note the mode of death. Ellicott: "Peter represents the Crucifixion as virtually the act of the rulers and people of Jerusalem and not of the Roman governor. The mode of death is described as in Deuteronomy 28:26... and in Galatians 3:10." The "tree" recalls the curse of the law (Galatians 3:13).

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes the two senses of "witness": Peter and the apostles were historical witnesses (eyewitnesses of the facts) and appointed witnesses (commissioned to testify and bring men to faith). He notes Peter touches the death briefly (openly known) but dwells on the resurrection (more doubtful, more available to faith).

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter appealed to the apostles as eyewitnesses of all Jesus did in Judea and Jerusalem (as at Pentecost, 2:32, Robertson), and stated the crucifixion — "whom they slew and hanged on a tree." The crucifixion is laid to the rulers and people of Jerusalem (Ellicott); the "tree" recalls the curse of the law (Deuteronomy 21:23; Galatians 3:13). Calvin distinguishes the apostles as both historical witnesses (eyewitnesses) and appointed witnesses (commissioned to testify), noting Peter touches the death briefly but dwells on the resurrection. The verse displays the apostolic foundation of the gospel: the eyewitness testimony of those who saw Jesus' works and his death. Peter preaches not hearsay but what he himself witnessed. The verse marks the testimony to the crucifixion: the Savior who went about doing good (v.38) was slain and hanged on a tree by his own people. The contrast between his beneficent life and his shameful death sets the stage for the resurrection (v.40), God's vindication of the one men crucified. Peter, an eyewitness, testifies to both the life and the death — and to the resurrection that he and the chosen witnesses saw, the heart of the gospel he preaches.
40:
"Him God raised up the third day, and shewed him openly;" The witnesses note the resurrection and its manifestation. The grammatical voices note the resurrection ascribed to God (the Father). Gill: "though it is sometimes ascribed to himself, as God... yet generally to God the Father, as here." The third day fulfills the Scriptures (1 Corinthians 15:4).

On "shewed him openly," Barnes notes the manifestation: "Manifestly; so that there could be no deception, no doubt of his resurrection." Clarke notes the timing: Jesus "lay long enough under the power of death to prove that he was dead; and not too long, lest it should be supposed that his disciples had time to practice some imposture." The third day was the divinely chosen interval.

The resurrection the witnesses read as the heart of the gospel and God's vindication of Christ. The one whom men slew and hanged on a tree (v.39), God raised the third day and showed openly — the proof that Jesus is the Christ and Lord of all. The resurrection is the foundation of the apostolic testimony.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: God raised Jesus the third day (the resurrection ascribed to the Father, fulfilling the Scriptures, Gill, 1 Corinthians 15:4) and showed him openly — manifestly, beyond doubt or deception (Barnes). The third day was the divinely chosen interval: long enough to prove his death, not so long as to allow the charge of imposture (Clarke). The verse displays the heart of the gospel: God's vindication of the crucified Christ in the resurrection. The one whom men slew, God raised and manifested — the proof that Jesus is the Christ and Lord of all (v.36). The verse marks the central fact of the apostolic preaching: the resurrection, attested by the chosen witnesses (v.41), the foundation of faith and the seal of God's approval upon his Son. The resurrection is God's answer to the crucifixion: man's "no" to Jesus overturned by God's "yes" in raising him. Peter preaches the risen Christ — raised the third day, shown openly to witnesses — as the ground of the salvation he offers the Gentiles. The resurrection, the heart of the gospel, is the proof that the crucified Nazarene is the living Lord and Judge of all.
41:
"Not to all the people, but unto witnesses chosen before of God, even to us, who did eat and drink with him after he rose from the dead." The witnesses note the chosen witnesses of the resurrection and the proof in eating and drinking. On "not to all the people," the witnesses note God's wisdom. Clarke gives reasons: showing him to all would cause tumult and confusion; God chose unimpeachable witnesses who knew him best, who would risk their lives to attest the fact. Robertson notes "the historian's candour" (Paley): Peter states the thing as it happened, not claiming foes saw him.

On "chosen before of God," the grammatical voices note the appointed witnesses (Robertson, Ellicott). The resurrection was attested not by a promiscuous multitude but by witnesses divinely appointed and prepared.

On "did eat and drink with him," the witnesses note the crucial proof. Barnes: "by doing this he furnished the clearest possible proof that he was truly risen; and that they were not deceived by an illusion of the imagination, or by a phantasm" (cf. Luke 24:41-43; John 21:13). Calvin marvels at Christ's condescension in eating to confirm the disciples' faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the risen Christ was shown not to all the people but to witnesses chosen before by God, who ate and drank with him after the resurrection. God's wisdom in choosing unimpeachable witnesses (rather than a promiscuous multitude that would cause tumult) is noted by Clarke; and the "historian's candour" — Peter not claiming foes saw him — adds to the credibility (Robertson, Paley). The eating and drinking is the crucial proof that the risen Christ was no phantom or illusion (Barnes, cf. Luke 24:41-43); Calvin marvels at Christ's condescension in eating to confirm the disciples' faith. The verse displays the firm foundation of the resurrection testimony: chosen, prepared witnesses, who had the clearest possible proof in eating and drinking with the risen Lord. The verse marks the apostolic certainty of the resurrection: not a vision or hallucination but the bodily risen Christ, seen, touched, and eaten with. Peter preaches the resurrection on the testimony of those who could not be deceived — the appointed witnesses who shared meals with the risen Savior. The candour and concreteness of the testimony confirm its truth: the gospel rests on the witnessed fact of the resurrection, attested by men who ate and drank with the living Christ.
42:
"And he commanded us to preach unto the people, and to testify that it is he which was ordained of God to be the Judge of quick and dead." The witnesses note the apostolic commission and Christ as Judge. On the command to preach, the grammatical voices note its source. Robertson takes Jesus as the subject (cf. 1:4), though some take God (Page). The apostles were commanded to preach and testify (cf. Matthew 28:19-20).

On "Judge of quick and dead," the witnesses note the kingdom and judgment of Christ. Barnes: "the living. The doctrine of the New Testament is that those who are alive when the Lord Jesus shall return... shall be caught up... without seeing death" (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17). Christ is ordained Judge of all, living and dead. Robertson: "Peter's claim for Jesus is that he is the Judge of Jew and Gentile (living and dead)."

The Reformed Calvin notes the kingdom of Christ in his being Judge: "the government of heaven and earth, and the perpetual government of the Church, are due to him." The "testify" carries weight, for men are inclined to unbelief and need God's strong protestation to believe the coming judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostles were commanded to preach and testify that Jesus is ordained by God to be the Judge of the living and the dead (cf. Matthew 28:19-20). "Quick and dead" means all men — those alive at Christ's coming (caught up without seeing death, 1 Thessalonians 4:16-17, Barnes) and those who have died; Christ is Judge of Jew and Gentile alike (Robertson). Calvin notes the kingdom of Christ in his being Judge — the government of heaven, earth, and the church due to him. The verse displays the exaltation of Christ as universal Judge: the crucified and risen one is ordained to judge all men. The verse marks a key element of the apostolic gospel: the lordship and judgment of Christ. The Savior who went about doing good (v.38) and was raised (v.40) is also the appointed Judge of all — a truth that presses upon the hearers the necessity of faith and obedience. Peter preaches Christ not only as the gracious Savior but as the coming Judge, before whom all must stand. The universal lordship (v.36) issues in universal judgment: he who is Lord of all will judge all. The gospel offered is also a summons to prepare for the Judge — and the way of preparation is faith in his name (v.43).
43:
"To him give all the prophets witness, that through his name whosoever believeth in him shall receive remission of sins." The witnesses note the prophetic testimony and the way of salvation. On the prophets, the grammatical voices note the appeal to Scripture. Clarke cites Isaiah, Jeremiah, Daniel, Micah, Zechariah; Christ is the sum of the law and the prophets, all bearing witness to him.

On "through his name whosoever believeth," the witnesses note the way of salvation by faith. Robertson: "no race distinctions are drawn... Gentiles do not have to become Jews, but have only to believe in Jesus as Messiah... It was glorious news to Cornelius." Ellicott: "Not by submitting to the bondage of the Law... but by the simple act of faith in Christ, and in the power of His Name, they, Gentiles as they were, might receive that remission of sins."

The Reformed Calvin draws out the three things in this conclusion: that it is Christ's office to reconcile men to God by remission of sins; that we receive remission by faith; that this doctrine is no new invention but witnessed by all the prophets since the beginning. He notes that regeneration is comprehended under faith as its effect.

The thin comment matches the verse, the climax of the sermon. The witnesses agree: all the prophets witness that "through his name whosoever believeth shall receive remission of sins" — the way of salvation by faith in Christ. The prophets, the sum of whose testimony is Christ (Clarke), bear witness to this. The "whosoever believeth" draws no race distinctions: Gentiles need not become Jews but have only to believe (Robertson, Ellicott) — glorious news to Cornelius. By the simple act of faith in Christ's name, Gentiles may receive the remission of sins their conscience sought. Calvin draws the three points: Christ's office to reconcile by remission of sins, the reception of it by faith, and the witness of all the prophets to this since the beginning. The verse displays the heart of the gospel and the answer to the seeking Gentiles: remission of sins through faith in Christ's name, offered to all who believe. The verse marks the climax of Peter's sermon: the offer of forgiveness to whosoever believes, Jew or Gentile, on the testimony of all the prophets. This is the answer to Cornelius's seeking (v.6) — not the law and circumcision, but faith in Christ for the remission of sins. And as Peter spoke these words, the Spirit fell (v.44) — God himself confirming the gospel of faith offered freely to the Gentiles.
44:
"While Peter yet spake these words, the Holy Ghost fell on all them which heard the word." The witnesses note the outpouring of the Spirit on the Gentiles — the "Gentile Pentecost." Chrysostom and the witnesses note the remarkable timing: the Spirit fell while Peter yet spoke, before baptism, before the laying on of hands — God's own attestation that the Gentiles were received. The puritan Henry marks the sovereign, anticipatory grace.

On the significance, the grammatical voices note this was a second Pentecost. Robertson (under v.45): "This sudden manifestation of the Holy Spirit's power on uncircumcised Gentiles was probably necessary to convince Peter and the six brethren that God had opened the door wide to Gentiles." The Spirit fell to settle the question of the Gentiles' admission beyond dispute.

The witnesses note the divine initiative. The Spirit fell not after baptism (the usual order) but while Peter preached — God himself anticipating the church's action and declaring his acceptance of the Gentiles. The outpouring confirmed the gospel of faith (v.43): those who believed the word received the Spirit at once.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: while Peter yet spoke, the Holy Spirit fell on all who heard — the "Gentile Pentecost," God's own attestation that the Gentiles were received. The remarkable timing (before baptism, before the laying on of hands) marks the sovereign, anticipatory grace of God (Henry), confirming the gospel of faith (v.43): those who believed received the Spirit at once. The outpouring was necessary to convince Peter and the six brethren that God had opened the door to the Gentiles (Robertson) — settling the question beyond dispute. The verse displays the divine initiative in the Gentile mission: God himself, by pouring out the Spirit, declares his acceptance of the believing Gentiles, anticipating the church's action. The verse marks the climactic moment of the chapter: the Spirit falling on the uncircumcised, the same gift as at Pentecost (2:4), proving that God makes no distinction between Jew and Gentile. The "Gentile Pentecost" is God's seal upon the gospel preached to Cornelius — the decisive evidence, which Peter would later use (11:15), that the Gentiles are to be received into the church on faith alone. The Spirit's descent, while Peter yet spoke, is the divine ratification of the door now opened to all nations.
45:
"And they of the circumcision which believed were astonished, as many as came with Peter, because that on the Gentiles also was poured out the gift of the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note the astonishment of the Jewish believers. The grammatical voices note the six brethren's amazement. Robertson: "They stood out of themselves." Clarke explains the cause: "it was a maxim with them that the Divine influence could not be revealed to any person who dwelt beyond the precincts of the promised land. Nor did any of them believe that the Divine Spirit could be communicated to any Gentile."

On the testimony, Ellicott notes Luke dwells on this as unimpeachable evidence: "Luke obviously dwells on this as a testimony, beyond suspicion, to the reality of the gift. Those who came with Peter were apparently not sharers at the time in the exultant joy... They listened with amazement." Here was the fulfillment of Peter's vision: the Gentiles, like the clean and unclean animals, taken up to heaven, no longer common or unclean.

The Reformed Calvin notes the believers' lingering error and God's patience: "He calleth them faithful who were as yet possessed with a wicked error... the Lord doth not by and by wipe away all clouds of ignorance from his." Their astonishment shows how hard it is to wind out of long-held errors.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Jewish believers who came with Peter were astonished that the Spirit was poured out on the Gentiles — for they held that the divine influence could not reach Gentiles (Clarke, Robertson). Luke dwells on their amazement as unimpeachable testimony to the reality of the gift (Ellicott): the six brethren, not at first sharing the joy, witnessed the undeniable outpouring. Here was the fulfillment of Peter's vision — the Gentiles, like the unclean animals taken up to heaven, no longer common. Calvin notes the believers' lingering error and God's patience, showing how hard it is to abandon long-held prejudices. The verse displays the decisive evidence and the providential witnesses: the very men whose Jewish prejudice would have doubted the Gentiles' admission were made eyewitnesses of God's gift to them. The verse marks the convincing proof: the Spirit poured out on the Gentiles, witnessed by astonished Jewish believers who could not deny it. Their amazement is itself the testimony that would silence the objections of the church (11:18). God, by pouring out the Spirit before these witnesses, established beyond dispute that the Gentiles are received — overcoming even the deep-rooted prejudice of the circumcised believers.
46:
"For they heard them speak with tongues, and magnify God. Then answered Peter," The witnesses note the evidence of the Spirit's gift — speaking with tongues. The grammatical voices note the parallel with Pentecost. Robertson: "new and strange tongues also as in 2:4, 11... This sudden manifestation of the Holy Spirit's power." The Gentiles spoke with tongues, the same sign given to the Jews at Pentecost.

On "magnify God," the witnesses note the content. Gill: "they spoke of the wonderful works of God in these languages... they spoke greatly in the praise of God." Clarke: "They had got new hearts as well as new tongues; and, having believed with the heart unto righteousness, their tongues made confession unto salvation."

The Reformed Calvin notes the purpose of the tongues: "the tongues were given them not only for necessity, seeing the gospel was to be preached to strangers, but also to be an ornament and worship to the gospel." He notes the later abuse of tongues (1 Corinthians) and how God withdrew the gift when corrupted.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the evidence of the Spirit's gift was that the Gentiles spoke with tongues and magnified God — the same sign given the Jews at Pentecost (2:4, Robertson). They magnified God, speaking his wonderful works and praise (Gill); they had new hearts as well as new tongues, their confession unto salvation (Clarke). Calvin notes the tongues were given both for necessity (the gospel to the nations) and as an ornament to the gospel, later abused (1 Corinthians) and withdrawn. The verse displays the undeniable evidence of the Gentiles' reception: the visible, audible sign of tongues, matching Pentecost, proving the Spirit had truly fallen on them. The verse marks the proof that convinced the Jewish believers (v.45): the Gentiles, speaking with tongues and praising God, manifested the same gift as the apostles. The tongues were God's public seal upon the Gentiles' faith — the evidence that they had received the Spirit and were received into the church. The same sign that marked the birth of the church at Pentecost now marks the opening of the church to the Gentiles: God makes no distinction, giving the like gift to those who believe, Jew and Gentile alike.
47:
"Can any man forbid water, that these should not be baptized, which have received the Holy Ghost as well as we?" The witnesses note Peter's argument for baptizing the Gentiles. The grammatical voices note the form (expecting the answer "no"). Robertson: "The evidence was indisputable that these Gentiles were converted and so were entitled to be baptized... The greater had been bestowed; could the lesser be withheld?" The gift of the Spirit was the warrant for baptism.

On the order (Spirit before water), Ellicott notes the exceptional case: "Ordinarily the gift of spiritual powers followed, by the laying on of hands, on the grace given in baptism. Now even that gift had been anticipated... While the history bore witness that the gifts of God may flow through other channels than the outward forms... it testified no less clearly that no spiritual gifts superseded the necessity of obedience to the law of Christ which had appointed those outward forms."

The Reformed Calvin reasons from the thing to the sign: "he which hath received the Spirit is also apt to receive baptism... the minister admit those unto the receiving of the outward sign whom God hath testified to be his children." He uses this for infant baptism (the children of believers being of God's household) and against the Papists who tie the Spirit's grace to the signs.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Peter argued that none could forbid water to baptize those who had received the Spirit "as well as we" — the gift of the Spirit the indisputable warrant for baptism (the greater bestowed, the lesser could not be withheld, Robertson). The exceptional order (Spirit before water) shows that God's gifts may flow through other channels than the outward forms, yet without superseding the necessity of obedience to Christ's appointed baptism (Ellicott). Calvin reasons from the thing to the sign (those whom God has testified to be his children are to receive the sign), using it for infant baptism and against the Papists who tie grace to the signs. The verse displays the conclusive argument: the Gentiles, having received the Spirit, are manifestly received by God and entitled to baptism. The verse marks the decision to admit the Gentiles fully into the church: the Spirit's gift settling the question, none could forbid them the water of baptism. The outpouring of the Spirit (v.44) is the ground; the baptism (v.48) is the seal. God has received the Gentiles; the church must not withhold the sign. Peter's argument establishes the principle that those whom God accepts and gifts are to be admitted to the church's ordinances — the door of baptism open to all who believe and receive the Spirit.
48:
"And he commanded them to be baptized in the name of the Lord. Then prayed they him to tarry certain days." The witnesses note the baptism of the Gentiles and the conclusion of the great event. On Peter not baptizing himself, the grammatical voices note he used the brethren. Barnes: "Why Peter did not himself baptize them is unknown. But it might be... to prevent the possibility of future cavil" (cf. 1 Corinthians 1:14-17). Ellicott notes Peter acted on the same principle as Paul, leaving the administration to others.

On the significance, Clarke marks the epochal moment: "This was properly speaking, the commencement of the Christian Church, as composed of Jews and Gentiles, partaking of the same baptism, united under the same Head... Now was the middle wall of partition broken down, and the Gentiles admitted to the same privileges with the Jews."

On "tarry certain days," Ellicott notes the fellowship that followed: Peter mingled freely with the new converts, eating and drinking with them (11:3) without fear of defilement — and he notes the contrast with Peter's later inconsistency at Antioch (Galatians 2:11-12), which Luke does not record here. Clarke draws the closing reflections on God's wonderful providence in the whole transaction.

The thin comment matches the verse, which closes the chapter. The witnesses agree: Peter commanded the Gentiles to be baptized in the name of the Lord (using the brethren, not baptizing himself, to prevent future cavil, Barnes, Ellicott, cf. 1 Corinthians 1:14-17), and tarried certain days. The baptism marks the epochal moment: the commencement of the church as composed of Jews and Gentiles, the middle wall broken down, the Gentiles admitted to the same privileges (Clarke). The "tarrying" shows the fellowship that followed — Peter eating and drinking freely with the new converts (11:3) without fear of defilement (Ellicott). The verse displays the completion of the great event: the Gentiles baptized, received fully into the church, and Peter remaining in fellowship with them. The verse marks the climax of the conversion of Cornelius: the door of faith opened to the Gentiles, the gospel and the church now embracing all nations. The God who arranged the converging visions, poured out the Spirit, and broke the old barrier now seals the work in the baptism of the Gentiles and the fellowship of Jew and Gentile in one body. The chapter that began with a God-fearing centurion ends with the church universal — Jew and Gentile, baptized into one Lord, partakers of the same grace. The middle wall is broken; the gospel is for all.
